title: Spin Peltier effect in graphene
authors: Xin Theng Lee, Xin Hu, Yuya Ominato, Masahiro Tatsuno, Takeo Kato, Mamoru Matsuo
abstract: In this work, we theoretically investigate the spin-Peltier effect in a heterostructure composed of graphene and a ferromagnetic insulator (FI). Using a microscopic formalism based on the characteristic spin-flip scattering length at the graphene/FI interface, we analyze how spin accumulation in graphene gives rise to a temperature difference across the junction. We show that, in the presence of an external magnetic field, the electronic spectrum of graphene is quantized into Landau levels, which strongly modifies the available spin-flip scattering channels. In particular, crossings between Landau levels significantly enhance the spin-flip scattering amplitude, leading to a pronounced amplification of the spin-Peltier response. Our results suggest that measurements of the spin-induced temperature difference in graphene-FI heterostructures can serve as a sensitive probe of discrete electronic energy levels. More broadly, this work provides a theoretical framework for understanding spin-driven thermal effects in hybrid systems combining Dirac materials and magnetic insulators.
summary: This paper theoretically explores how spin accumulation in a graphene/ferromagnetic insulator junction generates a temperature difference. It demonstrates that the resulting thermal response oscillates with the magnetic field, providing a way to use thermal measurements to probe Landau levels.
main_problem: Investigating the spin Peltier effect in graphene/ferromagnetic insulator heterostructures and determining if it can serve as a spectroscopic probe for Landau levels.
main_result: The spin Peltier response exhibits pronounced quantum oscillations as a function of the inverse magnetic field due to Landau level crossings, which can be used to detect discrete electronic energy levels.
method: A microscopic formalism using the Keldysh formalism and second-order perturbation theory to analyze spin-flip scattering at the interface.
model_or_system: A bilayer heterostructure consisting of monolayer graphene and a ferromagnetic insulator (FI) under a perpendicular magnetic field, modeled with a tight-binding Hamiltonian for graphene and a spin-wave approximation for the FI.
key_observables: Temperature difference (delta T) across the junction, spin current (jS), and energy current (jE).
important_parameters: Magnetic field (B), Landau level broadening (Gamma), interfacial exchange coupling (JS), and the temperature control parameter (JS/kBT).
main_assumptions: Strong spin relaxation in the FI, the spin-wave approximation for magnons, and the neglect of the Zeeman term for electrons in favor of a larger exchange bias.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the heterostructure; Figure 2 shows the junction schematic; Figure 3 demonstrates quantum oscillations in temperature difference vs. 1/B for different temperatures; Figure 4 shows the suppression of oscillations due to increased scattering rate.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the spin Peltier effect, defines the microscopic Hamiltonian and Keldysh-based formalism, derives the spin and energy currents, analyzes the temperature response under magnetic field quantization, and discusses the effects of temperature and broadening.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of a graphene/ferromagnetic- insulator (FI) heterostructure. An external magnetic field B is applied perpendicular to the graphene plane. The spin Peltier effect is induced by spin accumulation in graphene, which can be, for example, driven by the spin Hall effect in an adjacent normal metal.
FIG. 2. Schematic illustration of the FI-grphene junction.

FIG. 3. Quantum oscillation of the normalized temperature difference (∆T/δµs)norm = (∆T/δµs)/ max(∆T/δµs) plotted as a function of the inverse dimensionless magnetic field 1/B′. The lines correspond to different values of the temperature- control parameter JS/kBT = 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. The Landau- level broadening is fixed at Γ/JS = 1, with chemical potential µ/JS = 1. The oscillatory peaks originate from successive crossings of graphene Landau levels with the thermally active electronic window.

FIG. 4. Quantum oscillation of the normalized temperature difference (∆T/δµs)norm = (∆T/δµs)/ max(∆T/δµs) plotted as a function of the inverse dimensionless magnetic field 1/B′. The lines correspond to different values of the Landau-level broadening parameter Γ/JS = 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1. The tem- perature control parameter is fixed at JS/kBT = 10, with chemical potential µ/JS = 1. The oscillatory peaks originate from successive crossings of graphene Landau levels with the thermally active electronic window.
